notes: The product won't automatically do kernel updates, this could cause significant issues for customers who are not prepared for the kernel updates.

The product will not be controlling patch levels of every package installed on user's systems upgrading every package installed on a system without testing could be detrimental to the health of the system.
The product will not enable FIPS mode on any system, if customers are prepared to turn FIPS mode on, they will need to manually enable it.
The product cannot safely encrypt any partitions.
This rule only checks /etc/rsyslog.conf, however, the access methods will be tracked in /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/99_simp_local/99_remote_access_methods.conf in the format requested.
The product cannot make assumptions about the system's pki infrastructure and will not install trust chains automatically.
The product cannot make assumptions about the user's pki certificate structure and will not make assumptions about which certificates are being used and where.
Locking a user due to inappropriate encryption is not currently available in simp. A mechanism to address this rule will be implemented in a future release.
The product will not set any passwords for superusers.
The product will not set any passwords for superusers.
The product will not walk the entire filesystem looking for .keytab files, it could cause problems in systems leveraging large file shares.
We do not currently have a mechanism for scanning the filesystem for enforcement.
The product will not walk the filesystem looking for system commands. This could cause issues on systems utilizing large file shares.

The aide command will run with a cron job, but no assumptions can be made about where customers will want those reports to be sent to.
Enforcing this rule will only remove `!authenticate` from the /etc/sudoers file. Any custom files within /etc/sudoers.d will not be modified.

We do not currently have a mechanism for scanning the filesystem for enforcement.

The product parses the output of 'sshd -T' for the key that the system uses for communication and ensures that key is appropriately protected. The other keys in that location may not have the correct permissions applied. https://github.com/simp/pupmod-simp-ssh/issues/150 has been created to address this issue.

The product can't determine which accounts are temporary to set them to expire.
PKI infrastructure will vary heavily from organization to organization. The product won't manage any pki infrastructure.

The product cannot determine which accounts are necessary for any given system.
This check will fail if there is a commented out line that looks like "#PrintLastLog = <value>" even if the correct value of "PrintLastLog = true" exists in the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file.

There is no reliable way to remove extraneous umask commands without causing other errors. However, the umask that the product sets should take precedence over any others in the file since it will be located at the bottom.

There is no reliable way to remove extraneous umask commands without causing other errors. However, the umask that the product sets should take precedence over any others in the file since it will be located at the bottom.

The product cannot determine where audit aliases will be stored, the default location will be /etc/aliases, however, if we configure this and users configure the aliases elsewhere there will be no meaningful changes made.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.
The product cannot determine how much space is appropriate to store a week worth of audit logs. This will vary between organizations.
This rule only checks /etc/rsyslog.conf and /etc/sryslog.d/, however, the auth mode will be defined in /etc/rsyslog.simp.d/00_simp_pre_logging/global.conf.
The product cannot determine which chrony servers to sync to. This will vary between organizations.
Implementations to satisfy this rule will be highly system specific. The product cannot make assumptions about which ports and protocols should be allowed into any given system.

The scan rule will fail because the module being used currently places a space between the equals sign and the key/value being set in the logind.conf file. There is currently a pull request in the github module being used that should address this issue and prevent future failures - https://github.com/simp/pupmod-voxpupuli-systemd/pull/2.
The product currently has no way to determine which user accounts need to be have access to the sudo command.
